title: High-Alert Medications: Safe Handling
subtitle: The nurse safely handles high-alert drugs — doing independent two-nurse checks for insulin and heparin, never giving concentrated potassium IV push, separating look-alike drugs, and writing 'units' in full.

patient.name: High-Alert Medications
patient.admitDate: The nurse applies extra safeguards when giving high-alert medications.

vitals.temp: High harm if an error occurs

labs.v: Anticoag, K+, Insulin, Narcotics, Chemo, Heparin.

nursesNote: The nurse applies extra safeguards to high-alert medications, which carry a heightened risk of serious harm when given in error. Common classes are remembered as A-PINCH: Anticoagulants, Potassium and other concentrated electrolytes, Insulin, Narcotics (opioids), Chemotherapy, and Heparin. For drugs like insulin and heparin the nurse performs an independent double-check with a second nurse (verifying the drug, dose, concentration, pump settings, and patient) and writes 'units' out in full rather than 'U,' which can be misread as a zero and cause a tenfold overdose. Concentrated potassium chloride is NEVER given by IV push because it can cause fatal arrhythmias; it is diluted and infused slowly by pump (generally no more than 10 mEq/hr peripherally) with cardiac monitoring and IV-site assessment. Concentrated electrolytes are removed from floor stock, look-alike/sound-alike drugs are separated and labeled (Tall Man lettering), and error-prone abbreviations are avoided. The standard rights and three checks still apply on top of these measures.
providerOrders: Perform an independent two-nurse check for insulin and heparin
Never administer concentrated potassium chloride by IV push
Dilute potassium and infuse by pump with cardiac monitoring
Write 'units' in full and avoid error-prone abbreviations

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about high-alert medications?

opts.text: High-alert meds cause serious harm if given in error
Insulin, heparin, opioids, and potassium are high-alert
They often need an independent two-nurse check
Concentrated potassium chloride can be given by IV push
Look-alike/sound-alike drugs are stored apart
'Units' should be written out instead of 'U'
An independent double-check catches dosing errors before harm
Concentrated potassium IV push can cause fatal arrhythmias
Potassium must be diluted and infused slowly with monitoring
Abbreviating 'units' as 'U' can be misread as a zero
High-alert means the drug is used more often than others
Removing concentrated electrolytes from stock reduces risk
Verify the order and that it is diluted (never push)
Perform an independent double-check with a second nurse
Place on cardiac monitoring and assess the IV site
Infuse via pump at the ordered rate (<=10 mEq/hr)
Monitor for reactions and document
Insulin
Heparin
Oral acetaminophen
Concentrated potassium chloride
A daily oral multivitamin
IV morphine (opioid)
Oral docusate (stool softener)
Warfarin

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: An order reads 'give KCl 20 mEq IV push now'
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recognize that KCl is never given IV push
Hold the medication and do not administer it
Clarify the order with the prescriber
Give diluted KCl by infusion pump if the order is corrected
Document the clarification and action
Doing an independent two-nurse check for insulin
Giving concentrated potassium by IV push
Writing 'units' instead of 'U'
Storing look-alike drugs side by side, unlabeled
Infusing diluted potassium via pump with monitoring
Keeping vials of concentrated KCl in floor stock
Verifying heparin units with a second nurse
Skipping the double-check because the unit is busy
